Chapter 24
Lipids
©Evgeniy Ivanao/Getty Image: ©Bill grove/Getty image
© 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Lipids
Lipids are defined by their greater solubility in nonpolar solvents
Carbon-rich structures derived ultimately from glucose
Acetate, derived from pyruvate, is a key player in the biosynthesis of lipids

Section 24.1
ACETYL COENZYME A

Acetyl Coenzyme A
Coenzyme A acts as a carrier of an electrophilic acyl group throughout biosynthesis
Pyruvate is converted to acetyl coenzyme A via decarboxylation and oxidation
Nucleophilic addition, acyl substitution, and reactions at the α -carbon are all possible for acetyl CoA
Reactions of Acetyl CoA

Section 24.2
FATS, OILS, AND FATTY ACIDS

Fats and Oils: Triacylglycerols
Fats (solid) and oils (liquid) are an important class of lipids
They are triesters of glycerol, with very long hydrocarbon-containing acyl groups
Highly efficient for energy storage (ester hydrolysis is very exergonic )

Examples of Triacylglycerols
Note the long acyl groups !
Hydrogenation straightens out a “kink” in the chain, resulting in better packing and higher mp .

Fatty Acids
The long acyl groups in TAGs are derived from fatty acids , carboxylic acids with long hydrocarbon chains

Fatty Acids-1
May contain only C–C single bonds ( saturated ) or a few cis C=C double bonds ( unsaturated )

Fatty Acids-2
Most fatty acids contain an even number of carbons and unbranched chains

Trans Fats
Trans fats contain trans double bonds!
Partial hydrogenation can be accompanied by isomerization from cis to trans
Similar to saturated fats in their cholesterol-raising effect

Fatty Acids in Other Contexts
Fatty acids are mostly found in triacylglycerols
However, they can be found in other contexts too (bound to other heteroatoms )

Section 24.3
FATTY ACID BIOSYNTHESIS

Fatty Acid Biosynthesis
After some preparatory chemistry, a repeated series of nucleophilic acyl substitutions occurs
Four key molecules involved
Malonyl-ACP serves as an α -carbon nucleophile towards the growing acyl-ACP, which serves as an electrophile
Catalyzed by mammalian fatty acid synthase (mFAS )

Generation of Acyl-ACP Compounds
Malonyl coenzyme A is generated from bicarbonate and acetyl coenzyme A, via phosphorylation
Acyl substitution leads to replacement of –S(CoA ) with – S(ACP )

Generation of Acetoacetyl-ACP
Decarboxylation of malonyl-ACP gives a good nucleophile, which substitutes at C=O of acetyl-ACP
This is really a two-step process !

Generation of Saturated Acyl-ACP
Reduction of the “hanging” carbonyl group, dehydration, and reduction of C=C give a saturated acyl-ACP
The butanoyl-ACP (electrophile) then migrates to react with another malonyl-ACP (nucleophile)
Reduction-dehydration-reduction occurs again

Later “Pruning” Steps
Hydrolysis of the acyl-ACP generates the fatty acid
Other steps are possible:
Further chain extension
Addition of double bonds via radical oxidation and dehydration
Acyl transfer to form esters or amides
Acyl transfer may involve the intermediacy of a phospholipid (for example, in TAG synthesis )

Section 24.4
PHOSPHOLIPIDS

Phospholipids in TAG Biosynthesis
The species acylated in TAG biosynthesis is not glycerol itself, but glycerol phosphate

Phospholipids are Amphiphilic
Phospholipids contain a polar region and a nonpolar region
The polar regions point outward toward polar solvent; the nonpolar regions cluster in the center
The result is a lipid bilayer , a membrane separating two sides
The Fluid Mosaic Model

Section 24.5
WAXES

Waxes
Waxes are hydrophobic solids that form the outer coatings of many types of plants and animals
Commonly composed of fatty acid esters
For example, beeswax contains the highly hydrophobic ester triacontyl hexadecanoate
Earwax has similar properties

Section 24.6
PROSTAGLANDINS

Prostaglandins
Lipid fraction of semen contains compounds that exert profound influences on smooth muscle
The compounds responsible, prostaglandins , were later shown to be ubiquitous in animal tissues
Long-chain hydrocarbons containing a carboxylic acid and cyclopentane ring

Biosynthesis of Prostaglandins
Animals obtain the key precursor, linoleic acid , from plants
Chain extension and dehydrogenation give arachidonic acid
Cyclization and oxidation leads to PGG 2

Biosynthesis of Prostaglandins-1
Animals obtain the key precursor, linoleic acid , from plants
Chain extension and dehydrogenation give arachidonic acid
Other enzymes convert PGH2 to a variety of products
For example, one enzyme catalyzes O–O bond cleavage

Other Icosanoids
The C 20 carboxylic acid is icosanoic acid, and prostaglandins are known as icosanoids
Thromboxanes are a second class of icosanoids
Prostacyclins are a third class of icosanoids

Other Icosanoids-1
The C 20 carboxylic acid is icosanoic acid, and prostaglandins are known as icosanoids
Leukotrienes are not derived from PGH 2 , but come directly from arachidonic acid
